For IEC use only 48/618//DC 2026-04-03 INTERNATIONAL ELECTROTECHNICAL COMMISSION TECHNICAL COMMITTEE 48: ELECTRICAL CONNECTORS AND MECHANICAL STRUCTURES FOR ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC EQUIPMENT Document for comments on draft IEC Guide 126 “Guide in support of the development of product specific rules to assess the environmental footprint of IEC products” Background: The IEC Advisory Committee on Environmental Aspects (ACEA) was mandated by the SMB to develop a mandatory Guide entitled “Guide in support of the development of product specific rules to assess the environmental footprint of IEC products” for which the input of Technical Committees is requested, in particular if there are aspects that need to be added or changes brought to the first working draft of the Guide. All comments submitted by Technical Committees and other bodies will be considered by the ACEA in the preparation of the committee draft (CD), which will be submitted later to the SMB as per the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 1, A.4. IEC TC 48 National Committees are kindly invited to provide comments on the document attached in the Annex. The compilation of comments will then be forwarded to the ACEA Secretariat. The comments shall be submitted by using the IEC commenting template by 2026-05-15 at the latest. Jeffrey R. Toran Secretary of IEC TC 48 Annex: Working draft Guide 126 “Guide in support of the development of product specific rules to assess the environmental footprint of IEC products”

Attached you will find the first working draft of this guide, prepared by the ACEA TF Guide 126. All comments submitted will be considered in the preparation of the committee draft, which will be submitted later to the SMB for approval as per the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 1, A.4. Please share this information with your relevant subcommittees and working groups. Given the broad applicability and importance of this Guide, as well as the wider scope of ACEA’s work, we strongly encourage your committee to consider joining ACEA. Your participation would enable you to actively contribute not only to the development of this Guide but also to the many other significant activities undertaken by ACEA. ACTIONS: 1. Membership of ACEA: Please inform us whether your committee wishes to become a member of ACEA. If so, kindly provide the name of the representative who will participate on behalf of your committee. 2. Comments on Working Draft Guide 126: Please submit any comments or suggestions on the working draft using the IEC FormComments template and send them to the ACEA Secretariat (Suzanne Yap, sya@iec.ch and Nor Shahidah, nbi@iec.ch) by Wednesday, 2026-04-29 Please send any queries to the ACEA secretariat (sya@iec.ch). Annex: Working draft Guide 126

# Page 8^page=8
Draft IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 – 5 – INTRODUCTION 135 Today, humanity faces a complex, interconnected, and urgent environmental crises —the triple 136 planetary crisis —comprising the three interlinking issues of climate change, biodiversity loss, 137 and pollution. The demand on nature has exceeded the Earth’s biocapacity where the global 138 ecological footprint is estimated to be 1.7 times the earth's regenerative capacity today [ADD 139 REF]. 140 The cumulative environmental footprint on earth is directly linked to biodiversity loss, habitat 141 fragmentation and global climate change. The environmental footprint is used as a critical tool 142 for, for instance, policymakers and scientists to understand environmental impacts and plan for 143 sustainable development. Understanding environmental footprint and the respective 144 environmental impact allow for the quantification of product's contribution to e.g. global warming, 145 helping to monitor compliance with climate goals. 146 Obtaining information on carbon and other product environmental footprints is essential for 147 combatting climate change, ensuring corporate accountability, and promoting sustainable 148 development. By measuring and reporting these metrics, individuals and organizations can 149 identify the primary sources of greenhouse gas emissions (GHGs) and other environmental 150 impacts, allowing for targeted, data-driven strategies to reduce their product footprint and 151 promote a sustainable, low-carbon future. 152 The increased need to create transparency through the quantification and reporting of 153 environmental impacts of products, such as through environmental product declarations (EPD) 154 or carbon footprints of products (CFP) are connected with national and regional regulations or 155 other disclosure obligations for specific sectors, aiming for an environmentally sound decision 156 making. 157 With the intensification of the global challenges in combating climate change, IEC committees 158 have decided to take the next step and understand how their products contribute on the 159 environment by developing consistent methodologies (e.g. product category rules) to determine 160 for instance the carbon footprint of their products . 161 Product category rules (PCRs) provide sector-specific rules for calculating environmental 162 impacts (such as carbon footprint) for a category of products, e.g. electrical and electronic 163 products and systems (EEPS), along their entire life cycle. Moreover, PCRs provide minimum 164 requirements, as well as assumptions and scenarios for the assessment of environmental 165 impacts through a multiple-impact life cycle assessment (LCA) or the assessment of single 166 impact such as product carbon footprints, allowing for consistency amongst products. 167 Since the product category EEPS is very broad, diverse and complex, PCRs are often not 168 detailed enough to address products with different functions (e.g. vacuum cleaner vs. 169 refrigerator), different technology (e.g. lithium-ion EV batteries vs. NiMH batteries) or different 170 use cases (e.g. products that deliver the same function, but with different lifetime expectations). 171 Therefore, for product groups, product specific rules (PSRs) need to be applied instead. PSRs 172 allow for more detailed definitions and assessment. 173 This guide intends to support the IEC Committees to develop PSR standards for their product 174 group or subgroup based on the generic PCR for EEPS laid out in IEC 63366. 175 PCRs and PSRs can support a globally consistent and robust approach, allowing consistency 176 between environmental statements, such as EPD or CFP declarations, for different markets, 177 when utilized by respective programme operators. 178 179

– 6 – Draft IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 GUIDE IN SUPPORT OF THE DEVELOPMENT OF PRODUCT SPECIFIC 180 RULES TO ASSESS THE ENVIRONMENTAL FOOTPRINT OF IEC 181 PRODUCTS 182 183 184 1 Scope 185 This Guide assists IEC Technical Committees (TCs) and Subcommittees (SCs) responsible for 186 specific product groups to develop product specific rules (PSR) for the assessment of 187 environmental impacts of their products. 188 These product specific PSRs are to be developed based on IEC 63366 which defines product 189 category rules (PCR) for the entire electrical and electronic products and systems (EEPS) 190 category. 191 The PSRs provide the common rules for the LCA (life cycle assessment) carried out by product 192 manufacturers to quantify the environmental footprint (e.g. greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions) 193 from their products and prepare product-related environmental statements, such as self-194 declared environmental claims, environmental product declarations or carbon footprint of 195 products declaration. 196 2 Normative references 197 The following documents are referred to in the text in such a way that some or all of their content 198 constitutes requirements of this document. For dated references, only the edition cited applies. 199 For undated references, the latest edition of the referenced document (including any 200 amendments) applies. 201 – IEC 63366:2025, Product category rules for life cycle assessment of electrical and electronic 202 products and systems 203 – IEC 63372:2026, Quantification and communication of carbon footprint, GHG emission 204 reductions and avoided emissions from electric and electronic products and systems - 205 Principles, methodologies, requirements and guidance 206 – IEC Guide 121:2023, Securing credible environmentally relevant performance assessment 207 methods in standards 208 3 Terms, definitions and abbreviated terms 209 For the purposes of this document, the following terms and definitions apply. 210 ISO and IEC maintain terminological databases for use in standardization at the following 211 addresses: 212 – IEC Electropedia: available at http://www.electropedia.org/ 213 – ISO Online browsing platform: available at http://www.iso.org/obp 214 3.1 Terms and definitions 215 3.1.1 216 environmental statement 217 DEPRECATED: environmental claim 218 information on one or more environmental aspect(s) or environmental impact(s) of a product 219 which intends to inform an intended audience and intends to influence the market of this product 220 Note 1 to entry: The environmental statement can represent a point in time or can cover a period of time. 221

# Page 13^page=13
– 10 – Draft IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 3.2 Abbreviated terms 336 337 CFP Carbon footprint of a product CO2e/CO2eq Carbon dioxide equivalent DU Declared unit EEPS Electrical and electronic products and systems EoL End-of-life EPD Environmental product declaration FU Functional unit GLAD Global LCA data access network GHG Greenhouse gas GWP Global warming potential IPCC Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change TCs / SCs product technical committees and / or subcommittees LCA Life cycle assessment LCI Life-cycle inventory LCIA Life cycle impact assessment PECS Personalized environmental control systems PCR Product category rules PSR Product specific rules RSL Reference service life 4 Background and basic concepts 338 4.1 General 339 Earlier in this document we have explained that the cumulative environmental footprint on earth 340 is directly linked to biodiversity loss, habitat fragmentation, and climate change. 341 Obtaining and providing information on carbon and other environmental product footprints is 342 essential for combatting challenges such as climate change, ensuring corporate accountability, 343 and promoting sustainable development. By measuring and reporting these metrics, individuals 344 and organizations can identify the primary sources of product environmental impacts, allowing 345 for targeted, data-driven strategies to reduce their product footprint and promote a sustainable, 346 low-carbon society. 347 Environmental product footprint information—such as carbon footprint, water footprint, resource 348 depletion, or waste generation—is increasingly required across industries. Providing carbon 349 and other environmental footprint data is essential: 350 • To meet regulatory and market expectations ; 351 • In support of corporate sustainability strategies ; 352 • To enable transparency and trust; 353 • To provide the foundation for standardized reporting . 354 In essence, the PCR and the applicable LCA provides the environmental product footprint data 355 which is the input while EPDs and CFP declarations are the standardized outputs. Footprint 356 data without a standardized declaration is hard to compare. EPDs and CFP declarations turn 357 raw data into transparent, reliable, and comparable environmental information. 358 Environmental product footprint information becomes most valuable when it is consistent, based 359 on recognized standards and preferably verified by e.g. first, second or third-party verification 360 bodies. This is where product environmental statements, such as EPDs or CFP declarations, 361 become key. 362 NOTE 1 EPDs are designed to be transparent and do not include proprietary information, focusing instead on 363 disclosing environmental data that is essential for stakeholders, such as consumers, regulators, and industry 364 professionals. 365

# Page 14^page=14
Draft IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 – 11 – NOTE 2 Given that IEC standards are intended for global use, it is important to clearly establish that PCRs and 366 PSRs can be applied internationally, while still allowing justified regional adaptations where needed (e.g., differences 367 in energy mixes, usage patterns, end-of-life infrastructure, and selected impact categories or their assessment 368 methodologies). 369 4.2 Product environmental statements and the respective programmes 370 There exist several different types of product environmental statements. The ISO 14020 series 371 of standards set out the foundation for four types of product environmental statements, three of 372 which are relevant for this Guide, 373 • EPD (governed by ISO 14025), 374 • Footprint communication (governed by ISO 14026) , and 375 • Self-declared claims (governed by ISO 14021) . 376 For each type of product environmental statements, different requirements are stipulated and 377 defined. These requirements are collected in environmental statement programmes as shown 378 in Figure 1. 379 380 Figure 1 – Environmental impact assessment and disclosure 381 An environmental statement programme is a system that governs how environmental 382 statements about products are created, checked, and communicated. Most important, it makes 383 the information trustworthy by ensuring it is accurate, honest, and based on real data. 384

# Page 15^page=15
– 12 – Draft IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 In very simple words, an environmental statement programme is a system or set of rules that 385 tells companies how they should share environmental information about their products. It 386 explains: 387 • what environmental information needs to be shared, 388 • how it must be assessed and checked, 389 • and how it should be published, so people can trust it. 390 4.3 Environmental product statements 391 4.3.1 Environmental product declarations and carbon footprint of products 392 declarations 393 The disclosure of information on the environmental impacts of a product through an 394 environmental statement, such as environmental product declaration (EPD) or footprint 395 communication can be originated from, respectively: 396 • Multiple-impact life-cycle assessment covers the relevant environmental impacts for 397 the specific product category or product group under assessment. For example, 398 resource depletion, ecotoxicity and land use. 399 • Single-impact life-cycle assessment focus on a single environmental impact by a 400 product requiring a single-impact LCA. For example, climate change, which can be 401 derived from a carbon footprint-focused LCA. 402 NOTE LCA or footprint study based on PCR for self -declared environmental claims is not mandatory. For the sake 403 of transparency it is, however, recommended. 404 Multiple-impact LCAs are the start point for the preparation of a environmental product 405 declaration (EPD), while a single-impact LCAs lead to a single footprint assessment such as 406 carbon footprint of a product declaration (CFP declaration). 407 An environmental product declaration (EPD) is a standardized, 1st, 2nd or 3rd party 408 independently verified document that communicates the relevant environmental impacts of a 409 product over its life cycle (e.g., carbon footprint, energy use, water usage) . EPDs are derived 410 from life cycle assessments (LCAs) covering raw material extraction, production, transport, use, 411 and disposal according to the ISO 14040 and ISO14044 standards . EPDs are crucial for product 412 certification, sustainable procurement, and comparing product environmental impacts. More 413 details about EPDs are available in the next clauses. 414 CFP assessments and declarations, on the other hand, are a subset of an EPD that 415 specifically quantify the total greenhouse gas emissions (in 𝐶𝑂2 equivalents) produced 416 throughout a product's life cycle. A CFP declaration focusses solely on the "climate change" 417 impact, essential for carbon reduction targets and reporti ng. 418 A CFP declaration follows the ISO 14067 and IEC 63372 standards. While narrower than an 419 EPD, a CFP declaration is useful for buyers and regulators focused primarily on climate change 420 impacts. 421 4.3.2 EPD programme 422 EPDs are developed based on the requirements contained in an EPD environmental statement 423 programme. An EPD programme is a specific type of environmental product statement 424 programme focused exclusively on environmental product declarations (EPDs). – 24 – IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 Functional unit (FU) (incl. reference flow and declared unit) (cont.) PSR requirement specified in clause 4.2.2 of IEC 63366 are: • In PSR development it shall be taken into account that the definition of the RSL could lead to distortions of the usage scenarios between the products of the product group with a considerable range of product life span, where the environmental impacts woul d be underestimated for some products and overestimated for others. Hence this should be reflected in the FU and RSL, as well as the EPD, for example, by providing annualized data. • Each functional unit shall be further specified in PSR for each product or system family to improve comparison between assessment results. • Ref Figure 1 - Since this reference flow is very product (system) specific it shall be described in detail in PSR. • If relevant, the DU (declared unit) shall be specified further in the corresponding PSR. [IEC 63366|4.2.2] Since this reference flow is very product(system) specific it shall be described in detail in PSR. The reference flow represents the quantity of product, including all components, that is required to deliver the functional unit. This includes: • the physical product • accessories or components needed to perform the main function • consumables when they are essential for the basic operation of the product (e.g., filters, blades, lamps) • optional accessories are not included unless they defined as part of the product group scope. EXAMPLE 1 — Reference flow for a lighting product (generic LED luminaire) Functional unit: "Provision of 1,000 lumens of average lighting output for 10 years under typical indoor conditions." Reference flow: • One LED luminaire capable of delivering ≥1,000 lm at the defined performance level • Including integrated LED module (1 unit) • Including driver/power supply (1 unit) • Mounting accessories necessary for installation • No component replacement assumed during the RSL • Total mass of product: 1.2 kg • Materials: e.g., aluminium housing (0.7 kg), plastic optics (0.2 kg), PCB assembly (0.1 kg), wiring & connectors (0.05 kg), small parts (0.15 kg) NOTE This reference flow represents 100% of the physical product required to deliver the declared lighting service for 10 y ears. EXAMPLE 2 — Reference flow for a large appliance (e.g. refrigerator) Functional unit: "Maintain defined storage temperatures continuously for 10 years." Reference flow: • One refrigerator able to operate for 10 years • Refrigerant charge: e.g., 60 g R600a • 1 internal light source replacement assumed during RSL • Total mass: 65 kg • Materials: steel, plastics, insulation foam, compressor, PCB, etc. NOTE Reference flow represents the complete product + expected replacements. [IEC 63366|4.2.2] If relevant, the declared unit (DU) shall be specified further in the corresponding PSR. The declared unit must follow the functional unit and express performance per one product delivering the specified function over the specified RSL. EXAMPLE 1 — Declared unit for a luminaire: “1 indoor luminaire delivering 2,000 lumens of maintained illuminance over 50,000 hours at standard indoor ambient conditions.” Includes: • one luminaire • delivered, maintained lighting performance • over the RSL EXAMPLE 2 — Declared Unit for a battery‑powered product: “1 battery‑powered device delivering 150 use cycles per year for 5 years, including one battery replacement.” This makes the battery replacement explicit (required for correct modelling).

Draft IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 – 25 – Reference service life (RSL) RSL is the number of years, hours, or cycles a product is expected to reliably deliver its intended function under typical use conditions. It defines the time period over which a product is assumed to operate when calculating its environmental impacts. The reference service life (RSL) shall be assigned to each product group based on: • the technical characteristics of products on the market • failure modes and durability of key components • regulatory or standardization requirements • typical user expectations • availability of repair, maintenance, and spare parts The RSL must: • represent a reasonable, evidence‑based lifetime for the reference product • be applicable to the majority of products in the group • be used consistently in all calculations involving the functional unit EXAMPLE 1 – Lighting Products Product Type Typical RSL LED non-directional lamps 10,000 – 15,000 hours LED directional lamps 15,000 – 25,000 hours LED indoor luminaires 50,000 hours (often 50,000–70,000 h at L70/B50) EXAMPLE 2 – ICT & Consumer Electronics Product Type Typical RSL Laptop 5 years Desktop PC 5–7 years Smartphone 3–5 years Tablet 4–5 years Computer monitor 5 years Television 7 years Router/modem 5 years Printer 5–7 years EXAMPLE 3 – Household appliances (white goods) Appliance Typical RSL Washing machine 10–12 years Dishwasher 10 years Refrigerator/freezer 12 years Tumble dryer 10 years

– 28 – IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 System boundary and life cycle stages The system boundary requirements set out in 4.2.3 of IEC 63366:2025 apply. The life cycle of the EEPS under study shall at least cover the following main life cycle stages: a) manufacturing stage, including relevant upstream process (e.g. acquisition of raw material, components/parts manufacturing, assembly etc.) and the main manufacturing and processing steps; b) distribution stage; c) use stage, including potential steps (repair, maintenance, update, upgrade, and refurbishing) within the RSL associated with the reference product; d) end-of-life stage, including the necessary steps until and for the final disposal, recovery (for example, energy) or recycling of the product or the product system. Additionally, the following life cycle stages can be relevant and should be considered in the LCA: e) installation stage, including the relevant steps for the preparation of the product for use; f) de-installation, including the relevant steps for the preparation of the product for end-of-life. PSR requirements are additionally specified in clause 4.2.3 in IEC 63366 are: • Further specifications concerning the system boundary shall be defined in the PSR. • Details concerning cut-off criteria may be developed, defined and justified in PSR based on for example conducted LCA case studies or compiled from literature and research papers. [IEC 63366|4.2.3.1] Further specifications concerning the system boundary shall be defined in the PSR. The system boundary defines which processes, life -cycle stages, the inputs and outputs, activities that included in (or excluded from) the assessment of a product or product system. For instance: • Which life‑cycle stages are included (e.g., raw materials acquisition and processing, manufacturing, distribution, installation/deinstallation, use stage, end ‑of‑life) • Which processes inside those stages are counted (e.g., electricity used in production, transport distances, maintenance, software updates, disposal methods) • Which inputs and outputs related to the production of the materials and components are part of the system (e.g. energy, materials, emissions, replacements, consumables (filters, batteries, lamps) • What is NOT included (e.g. cleaning, rare accessories, business travel, or user behaviour outside the product’s function, non-representative repairs). Different EEPS have different manufacturing steps, different use‑phase characteristics, different maintenance cycles, different end‑of‑life processes and different consumables or replacement needs and therefore, the system boundary cannot be “one -size-fits- all”. The PSR must specify the exact processes, flows, and life‑cycle stages that are included for the specific product group. EXAMPLE 1 — Lighting Products (Lamps & Luminaires) The system boundary includes the following life -cycle stages: • Raw materials: aluminium housings, LEDs, drivers, optics, plastics, glass • Manufacturing: SMT processes, LED board production, driver assembly, luminaire housing manufacturing • Distribution: global shipping + domestic trucking, packaging • Installation: if installation materials (e.g., brackets, cables) are integral to functionality • Operation: ­ Electricity use overrated lifetime ­ Light output depreciation over time (if relevant) ­ Driver replacement (if required during RSL) • Maintenance: ­ Cleaning ­ Lamp replacement (for luminaires with replaceable lamps) • End‑of‑Life: ­ Collection via WEEE systems ­ Removal of batteries (for emergency luminaires) ­ Recycling of aluminium, steel, PCBs ­ Special handling of mercury lamps (if present) ­ Energy recovery of plastics Excluded: • Optional decorative components not needed for function • Additional control gear not included in the base product • Building‑level lighting control systems EXAMPLE 2 — ICT equipment (Laptop, monitor, desktop PC) The system boundary includes the following life -cycle stages: • Raw material production (casing plastics, metals, PCB, battery materials) • Component fabrication (CPU, memory, screen, HDD/SSD) • Product assembly and testing • Packaging and global distribution • Use-phase electricity consumption (active, idle, sleep, off-mode) • Software updates that affect energy use • Battery replacement (if required in RSL)

– 30 – IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 Life cycle inventory Additional PSR requirements in clause 4.2.4 in IEC 63366 are: • The relevance of life cycle stages and their underlying processes shall be defined further in corresponding PSR. • [installation] This stage shall be considered separately only when a specific installation process is defined for the respective product, and should be defined in PSR. • [deinstallation] This stage shall be considered separately only when a specific process is defined for the respective product, and should be defined in the PSR. A PSR often states that all relevant life‑cycle stages must be considered—but the real value comes from defining how relevance is determined and what processes must be included. Below some practical guidance: Define “Relevance” as a transparent and logic decision A PSR should explicitly define relevance as: A life ‑cycle stage or process is considered relevant when it can influence the occurrence, introduction, amplification, or detection of product safety risks. Apply a systematic relevance‑assessment method by e.g. asking the following questions: Q1 — Does this stage introduce or modify hazards? Examples: design errors, manufacturing defects, software updates. Q2 — Can failures in this stage lead to unsafe conditions during use? Examples: inadequate maintenance instructions, transport damage. Q3 — Does this stage contribute to risk controls or mitigation? Examples: production testing, installation procedures, calibration. Q4 — Could misuse or foreseeable behaviour during this stage affect safety? Examples: end‑of‑life disposal, user modifications. A stage is considered relevant if yes to any of the above. The PSR should include a life‑cycle breakdown. A robust PSR should require explicit consideration of stages such as: • Concept / requirements definition • System architecture & design • Component selection • Manufacturing & assembly • Factory inspection & testing • Transport & storage • Installation & commissioning • Operation (normal + reasonably foreseeable misuse) • Maintenance & repair • Software updates / configuration changes • Decommissioning & disposal NOTE: Not all will be relevant, but all must be screened. The PSR should include a life‑cycle relevance matrix, as relevant, e.g.: Life-cycle stage Hazard introduction potential Impact on risk controls Impact on user behavior Manufacturing Yes – soldering defects Yes – test coverage No Operation Yes – misuse possible Yes – protective circuits Yes Disposal No No No Linking relevance to required safety activities - The PSR should define that for each relevant life‑cycle stage, appropriate safety/risk analysis, controls, validation methods, and documentation must be applied. Examples: • Design → hazard analysis, FMEA, architecture decisions • Manufacturing → process controls, end‑of‑line testing • Transport → robustness, packaging requirements, drop tests • Operation → safe‑use instructions, warnings, user interface design • Maintenance → service procedures, lock‑out requirements NOTE: This ties relevance to concrete obligations.

Draft IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 – 31 – Allocation rules Additional PSR requirements in clause 4.2.5 in IEC 63366 are: • [between co-products] The allocation rule for co-products should be defined further in PSR, in accordance with allocation principles defined in ISO 14044. • [recovery operation] The allocation rule for recovery operations should be defined further in PSR, in accordance with allocation principles defined in ISO 14044. Here below are examples of allocation rules for co ‑products to use in a PSR 1. Mass‑based allocation (physical relationship) Rule: If co‑products are generated from the same process and mass differences reflect production effort or burden, allocate impacts proportionally by mass. Example: A biorefinery produces 70 kg pulp and 30 kg lignin Allocation: 70% of upstream impact allocated to pulp, 30% to lignin. 2. Energy content allocation (calorific value) Rule: When co‑products have different energy contents and are intended as energy carriers, allocate by lower heating value (LHV). Example: Waste treatment produces: 10 MJ syngas and 20 MJ heat Allocation: based on 1/3 syngas, 2/3 heat. 3. Functional relationship allocation (causality) Rule: Allocate based on the purpose or function that drives the process. Example: A CHP (combined heat and power) plant: Electricity is primary function and Heat is secondary Allocation: using exergy or functional priority (e.g., 70% to power, 30% to heat). 4. Economic allocation Rule: If physical relationships do not reflect value or causality, allocate by market value. Example: Outputs: 1 kg high‑grade chemical (€10/kg) and 9 kg low‑grade by-product (€0.5/kg). Total value = €10 + €4.5 = €14.5 Allocation: 10 / 14.5 = 69% to high‑grade chemical and 4.5 / 14.5 = 31% to low‑grade by-product 5. System expansion / substitution (allocation avoidance) Rule: Avoid allocation by expanding the system to include displaced products. Example: A process produces: • Main product A • Co‑product B that replaces product C on the market Allocation: subtract the environmental burden of C (“credits system”). 6. Subdivision of processes Rule: Where feasible, separate shared and non‑shared process steps and individually assign them. Example: A chemical plant shares cleaning and heating, but separation and packaging are product -specific. Allocation: Shared steps allocated; specific steps directly assigned.

– 32 – IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 Scenarios development Scenarios describe the parameters of a specific function or process of the product system. The development of scenarios is based on 4.3 of the IEC 63366:2025. [IEC 63366|4.3.3] – Use scenarios The use scenario shall define at least: ­ the reference service life (RSL); ­ the ambient conditions and operating profile or load factor or utilization factor (for example, percentage use rate during the RSL), or all of them. The reference set of conditions defining the ambient and operating conditions and any other conditions associated with the RSL, can be defined in PSR, specific product standard or the instruction for use of the product; and ­ if applicable, the maintenance conditions as specified by the manufacturer to fulfil the RSL (frequency, replacement parts, consumables, etc.). Additional PSR requirement in clause 4.3 of IEC 63366 are: • Scenarios describe the parameters of a specific function or process of the product system. Specific scenarios shall be developed based on the stipulations set out in 4.3 and should be laid down in corresponding PSR. • Specific data should be taken into account for transportation: number of kilometres, types of transport and payload factor. The scenario should be detailed in a PSR. • Use scenarios shall be specified in PSRs as they are very product specific. The long-term averages of actual use are often a more accurate source than laboratory measurements in relation to actual impacts. However, for comparative reasons, theoretical valu es are sometimes preferred. Which applies should be specified by PSR. • The use scenario shall define at least: o the reference service life (RSL); In addition to the general requirements set out in IEC 63366, the detailed requirements per product type are defined in the respective Annexes. If the manufacturer wishes to use specific data, this data shall be justified in the LCA report and indicate in the EPD. This data shall be based on evidence along the chain of custody. Collecting and archiving supporting documents are the responsibility of the issuer of the EPD and must be available if reques ted [IEC 63366|4.2.4.1] The relevance of life cycle stages and their underlying processes shall be defined further in corresponding PSR. Scenario development covers: use, maintenance, installation, distribution, repair, and end ‑of‑life (EoL). All of these have elements that depend heavily on product group characteristics and must therefore be defined by the PSR. Below are the aspects that mus t be defined for product group PSRs because they cannot be harmonized across the full EEPS c ategory. • Use patterns (hours, cycles, load, energy use) • Reference Service Life (RSL) • Maintenance and repairs (frequency, parts, labour) • Consumables and replacements • Distribution & logistics • Installation and de‑installation • End‑of‑life flows (collection, depollution, recycling) • Component-specific scenarios (batteries, drivers, filters, PCBs, lamps) • Performance degradation assumptions (light depreciation, battery aging) • User behaviour assumptions (household vs. professional use) Transportation (distribution?) scenario [IEC 63366|4.3.2] Specific data should be taken into account for transportation: number of kilometres, types of transport and payload factor. The scenario should be detailed in a PSR. Because transportation patterns depend on size, weight, fragility, value, and market routes, scenarios are structured by prod uct- group characteristics rather than specific product type. EXAMPLE 1 Small, light products (≤ 2 kg; small ICT, handheld tools, small appliances) Typical transport scenario: ­ Factory → Regional distribution center: Long‑distance truck or air freight for high ‑value goods ­ Distribution center → Retailer or direct-to-consumer: Short‑distance truck or courier ­ E‑commerce shipping common ­ Packaging: Single‑unit packaging; parcel shipments Reason: products are small enough for parcel networks and sometimes air freight, especially for consumer electronics. Representative scenario ­ 8,000–12,000 km sea freight or air freight alternative (Asia → EU) ­ 800 km long‑haul truck ­ 150 km regional distribution ­ 20 km last-mile delivery EXAMPLE 2 Large household appliances (> 20 kg; fridges, washing machines, AC outdoor units): ­ Almost always ship freight for intercontinental routes ­ Heavy, bulky items transported on pallets ­ Final delivery often includes two-person handling Representative scenario is: ­ 20,000 km shipping container ­ 1,500 km regional trucking ­ 50 km home delivery (large vehicle; sometimes white‑glove delivery)

– 36 – IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 Data selection and quality Data selection and data quality requirements shall be followed based on 4.2.7 of the IEC 63366:2025. Primary data sources, specific data or average data derived from specific production processes shall be the first choice as a basis for the LCA. In addition, the following rules apply in case the LCA results are intended for an environmental declaration: a) An LCA of an average product shall be based on representative average data for the declared product, at least for the processes the producer has influence on. NOTE Average product depends on the LCA study's goal and scope, so an average product can be an average between different manufacturers for one specific power rating (functional unit) or an average product of one manufacturer in a typical configuration, for example, evaluated by market share. b) An LCA of a specific product shall be based on specific data for the declared product, at least for the processes the producer has influence on. c) Generic data may be used for some upstream and downstream processes, for example, raw material extraction, electricity generation, waste incineration and recycling processes. When specific data is not available, as secondary data source, generic data should be used for the processes included in the system boundaries. Generic data shall be identified and consistent with the scope of the study in terms of temporal, geographic and technological coverage. Additional PSR requirements in IEC 63366 clause 4.2.7 are: • Selection of primary data sources, specific data, average data, or generic data should be detailed further in PSR. [IEC 63366|4.2.7.1] Selection of primary data sources, specific data, average data, or generic data that are specific for a product group should/shall be detailed in PSR. Cherry-picking data is according to IEC 63366 not allowed. Examples of cherry-picking include selecting: • a supplier with unusually low impacts (ignoring the average market) • a transport route that is shorter than typical • a component recycling rate that is higher than industry reality • only the most efficient product settings, ignoring normal use • an unusually long lifetime to dilute impacts EXAMPLE 1 — Example of a scenario for LED Luminaire PSR that is Cherry‑picked (incorrect approach) A manufacturer selects: ­ only one supplier of LED drivers who claims extremely low environmental footprints, ­ even though most drivers used in the market have average impacts 30 –40% higher. The manufacturer uses this low‑impact supplier data in the LCA even though their real production uses a mix of suppliers. Why this is cherry-picking: ­ The selected data does not represent the actual supply chain. ­ The PSR requires market-average or production‑weighted data. ­ The LCA result becomes artificially lower and is not comparable to other manufacturers’ results. The correct approach (according to the PSR requirements): ­ a market-weighted average driver dataset, or ­ a manufacturer‑specific average across all suppliers used in real production. EXAMPLE 2 — Example of transport scenario cherry‑picking (Used often in EEE unless prevented by PSR) Why cherry‑picked data: The manufacturer models transport as 100 km regional truck ignoring the fact that the product is actually imported from Asia via: ­ 18,000 km ocean freight ­ 700 km truck transport The correct PSR rule: ­ The PSR needs to provide credible and representative transport distances and modes to avoid non -comparable / non- credible data choices.

Draft IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 – 37 – Additional rules Requirements on PSR in clause 4.5 of IEC 63366 are: • If extrapolation rules have already been set in the PSR, they may be directly applied. Extrapolation rules define when evidence from one product, component, or test case can be also applied to another product of a similar type, avoiding e.g. unnecessary testing while still being justified. Typical (auditable) examples are: 1: Dimensional similarity Rule: If the only design variation is size within ±10% of the tested configuration, the original safety test results may be e xtrapolated. Example: Drop-test results for a 100 mm device can be applied to a 95 –110 mm variant. Rationale: Mechanical stress does not significantly change within this tolerance. 2. Material equivalence Rule: Extrapolate results only when the new variant uses materials with equal or better safety -relevant properties (flammability, toxicity, tensile strength, etc.). Example: A housing tested in UL94 V‑0 plastic → acceptable for variants using the same or higher ‑rated (e.g., 5VA) material. 3. Functional similarity Rule: Variants with functionally identical subsystems can share safety evaluations if electrical and mechanical interfaces do not change. Example: Replacing a sensor with another model having the same voltage range, insulation category, and failure behavior. 4. Worst‑case bounding Rule: If one configuration is demonstrated to be the worst case, all configurations with lower risk or stress loads inherit t he result. Example: Testing thermal rise at maximum load current; lower ‑current variants are automatically covered. 5. Environmental equivalence Rule: Extrapolate environmental robustness results (IP rating, humidity tests, corrosion tests) only if the new configuration does not exceed the tested environmental envelope. Example: A product tested for −10 °C to +50 °C covers variants intended for 0 °C to +40 °C. 6. Software / firmware minor change Rule: Safety evidence can be extrapolated if software changes are classified as non ‑safety‑relevant (e.g., UI improvements). Example: Changing display colors does not require repeating functional safety tests. 7. Assembly variant Rule: For products manufactured in multiple locations, results may be extrapolated if assembly procedures, tooling, and quali ty controls are equivalent and documented. 8. Electrical equivalence Rule: A PSR may allow extrapolation when electrical characteristics remain within ±5% of the tested configuration. Example: A power module tested at 24 V ±10% covers a variant using 24 V ±5%. 9. Component tolerance Rule: If a change remains within component specification tolerances, no new testing is required. Example: Using a resistor with the same nominal value but a tighter tolerance. 10. Hazard invariance Rule: Extrapolation is allowed when a change does not introduce new hazards or change hazard severity. Example: Changing external color or label placement.

The standards 1029 ISO 14040 and 14044 describe the four main phases of an LCA: 1030 – Definition of goal and scope 1031 – Inventory analysis 1032 – Impact assessment 1033 – Interpretation 1034 Figure A.1 shows that these steps are interlinked and depend on each other , since LCA is an 1035 iterative methodology, where it is possible or rather necessary to refine things in context to the 1036 interpretation of the results. 1037 1038 Figure A.1 – Iterative steps of a life cycle assessment 1039 LCI (life cycle inventory) and LCIA (life cycle impact assessment) are crucial phases in LCA 1040 (life cycle assessment). LCI involves collecting data on all energy, raw materials, and emissions 1041 for a product. LCIA translates this raw inventory data into quantifiable environmental impacts 1042 (e.g., carbon footprint). 1043 For instance, the first round of analysis may tell that more data is needed. Or the results of the 1044 assessment or the interpretation may nudge to revise the goal and scope. In this sense, every 1045 LCA not only provides with valuable insights for business improvements but also guides the 1046 planning of the next LCA, enhancing learning and further decisions. 1047

– 46 – IEC CD Guide 126 © IEC:2026 With LCA, one can assess the product or service’s environmental effects from the first to the 1048 last stage or at any point in between. There are different scopes: ‘cradle to gate’ (from raw 1049 materials to the factory gate), ‘gate to gate’ (focusing solely on the manufacturing processes), 1050 or ‘cradle to grave’ (from raw materials to disposal). 1051 A.1.2 Definition of goal and scope 1052 The first step – goal and scope definition – sets the foundation by outlining the LCA’s purpose 1053 and the boundaries of the study. This ensures that the LCA is performed consistently. 1054 An LCA models a product, service, or system life cycle. A model is a simplification of a complex 1055 reality. As with all simplifications, this means that the reality will be distorted in some way. The 1056 challenge for an LCA practitioner is to make sure the simplification and distortions do not 1057 influence the results too much. The best way to accomplish this is to carefully and thoroughly 1058 define the goal and scope of the LCA study. 1059 The goal and scope describe the most important choices, which are often subjective. For 1060 instance, the reason for executing the LCA, a precise definition of the product and its life cycle 1061 and a description of the system boundaries. 1062 The system boundaries describe what is taken into the assessment and what is left out. For 1063 instance, small amounts of ingredients that contribute little to the total footprint can be left out 1064 of the scope of the study. Thus, the system boundaries exclude this item. 1065 A.1.3 Inventory analysis 1066 In the second step – inventory analysis of extractions and emissions – one look at all the 1067 environmental inputs and outputs associated with the product, service or system. Environmental 1068 input means something you take out of the environment to put into the product’s life cycle, for 1069 example, the use of raw materials and energy. Environmental outputs mean something the 1070 product’s life cycle puts or emits into the environment – for example, the emission of pollutants 1071 and waste streams. Together, this information gives the complete picture of the life cycle 1072 inventory (LCI). The LCI is all about collecting relevant data and modelling this data via inputs 1073 and outputs in a correct manner. 1074 A.1.4 Impact assessment 1075 Then, in the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA), the potential environmental impacts derived 1076 from the inventory analysis are evaluate. This allows to understand sustainability challenges 1077 better and make better business decisions. The environmental impacts of all processes 1078 collected and modelled in the LCI can be classified and translated into environmental themes 1079 such as global warming or human health. 1080 The most important choice to make is how integrated the results should be in the end. Should 1081 there be one single score to show how sustainable the product is or to see whether a new 1082 design improves on CO 2 emissions and how this impacts the land use? 1083 This choice usually depends on what is needed to address certain target audience and the 1084 ability of this audience to understand the detailed results. 1085 A.1.5 Interpretation 1086 The interpretation phase concludes the assessment by reviewing the conclusions and ensuring 1087 they are well-substantiated. The ISO 14044 standard describes several checks to test whether 1088 the data and the procedures used support the final conclusions. 1089 Especially when a LCA becomes more complex, continuously interpreting the results helps 1090 optimize the analysis as it goes further down the process. 1091

Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) – Potential contributions to stratospheric ozone layer thinning: • ODP is very low and primarily associated with upstream material supply chains (e.g., refrigerants or chemicals used indirectly in metal processing). • No operational contributions occur since the device contains no ozone ‑depleting substances. Acidification Potential (AP) – Emissions that lead to acid rain: • AP is driven mainly by SO₂ and NOₓ emissions associated with steel and copper production. • Transportation of heavy materials also contributes. Eutrophication Potential (EP) – Nutrient releases that can cause algae growth in water bodies: • EP impacts mainly arise from metal production effluents and transport emissions. • Operational contribution is negligible Photochemical Ozone Formation (POCP) Resource Use & Energy (Primary Energy Demand) – Consumption of renewable and non‑renewable primary energy: • Non‑renewable primary energy demand is high due to electricity and heat use in manufacturing and upstream material refining. • Renewable energy demand is moderate and mostly tied to background energy mixes in material production. Resource Use — Materials (Mineral & Metal Depletion): • Material resource depletion is dominated by large amounts of copper and steel in breaker components (contacts, mechanisms, housing). • Vacuum interrupters also contain refined metals with high embodied resource intensity. Water use: • Water use is primarily from indirect industrial processes (metal treatment, machining, cooling). • Direct manufacturing water use is relatively minor compared to upstream contributions. Waste & Output Flows – Tracks hazardous waste, non-hazardous waste, and materials sent for recycling or recovery. (Calculated per EN 50693 via LCA modules.) • Non‑hazardous waste dominates due to metal scrap and packaging from manufacturing. • Hazardous waste is minimal and mainly associated with specialized component fabrication. • At end‑of‑life, the product shows high recycling potential because most parts are steel and copper. 3.2 Summary of key environmental impact indicators (quantitative) • Total GWP: 725 kg CO₂‑eq (≈ similar order of magnitude to other MV switchgear EPDs) • Metals drive >80% of impacts across categories. • Use-phase impact: negligible (no operational energy). • End‑of‑life: >70% of mass recyclable. 1177 Category Summary (qualitative) Unit Total GWP-total High manufacturing impact due to steel, copper, and vacuum interrupter production kg CO₂-eq Cradle-to-Gate (A1-A3): 680 Use Stage (B1-B7): 0 ODP Very low (driven mainly by upstream chemical inputs) g CFC-11-eq 3.2×10⁻⁶
